1	---
2	name: orchestrated-execution
3	description: 4-phase execution loop for work units - IMPLEMENT, VALIDATE, ADVERSARIAL REVIEW, COMMIT
4	auto_activate: false
5	triggers:
6	  - "orchestrated execution"
7	  - "4-phase loop"
8	  - "adversarial review"
9	---
10	
11	# Orchestrated Execution Skill
12	
13	**Core principle**: Trust nothing. Verify everything. Review adversarially.
14	
15	This skill defines a generalized 4-phase execution loop that any orchestrator can invoke when implementing work units. It replaces linear "implement then review" flows with a rigorous cycle that independently validates results and adversarially reviews against a written spec contract.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Coordination Mode Note
20	
21	This skill is mode-agnostic вҖ” the 4-phase execution loop works identically in both Task Mode and Team Mode. The differences:
22	
23	- **Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT)**: In Team Mode, the coding subagent may be a persistent teammate (retains context across work units). In Task Mode, it's a fresh `Task()` per work unit.
24	- **Phase 3 (ADVERSARIAL REVIEW)**: ALWAYS a fresh `Task()` instance in BOTH modes. Never a teammate, never resumed.
25	- **All quality gates**: Unchanged regardless of mode.
26	
27	See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for full mode detection and coordination details.
28	
29	---
30	
31	## Plan Review Gate
32	
33	After drafting an implementation plan (Step 1: Plan Validation), submit it to the **Plan Review Gate** before presenting to the user. The gate spawns 3 adversarial reviewers (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) вҖ” all must PASS. See `skills/plan-review-gate/SKILL.md` for details.
34	
35	---
36	
37	## When to Use This Skill
38	
39	- **Complex tasks** decomposed into multiple work units
40	- **Tasks with a written spec** containing Definition of Done (DoD) items
41	- **Multi-agent orchestration** where subagents produce work that needs verification
42	- **High-stakes changes** where self-reported "it works" is insufficient
43	
44	**Do NOT use for**: Single-file bug fixes, copy changes, or tasks without a spec.
45	
46	---
47	
48	## 1. Plan Validation (Pre-Flight Checklist)
49	
50	Before submitting a plan to the Design Review Gate, the orchestrator MUST verify every item on this checklist. This prevents expensive design review cycles on fundamentally broken plans.
51	
52	> **Note**: The `Plan` subagent type cannot write files (it has read-only access by design). If you spawn an Architect as a Plan subagent, it will return the plan as text in its response. The orchestrator must write the plan to `PLAN.md` itself.
53	
54	### Architecture Checklist
55	- [ ] Every data access goes through a service layer (no direct DB calls from routes/handlers)
56	- [ ] Each work unit has a single responsibility (max ~5 files created/modified)
57	- [ ] Error handling strategy specified (typed error hierarchy, how errors cross layer boundaries)
58	- [ ] No hard-coded configuration вҖ” environment variables for all external service config
59	
60	### Dependency Graph Checklist
61	- [ ] Each WU's dependencies are minimal (only depends on what it actually imports/uses)
62	- [ ] No unnecessary serialization вҖ” WUs that CAN be parallel ARE marked parallel
63	- [ ] No circular dependencies
64	- [ ] Integration WUs exist to wire components into the app shell (not just built in isolation)
65	
66	### API Contract Checklist
67	If the plan includes HTTP endpoints or WebSocket protocols, verify:
68	- [ ] Every HTTP endpoint specifies: method, path, request schema, all response status codes, error response shapes
69	- [ ] WebSocket message types fully specified (client-to-server AND server-to-client message type tables)
70	- [ ] Protocol concerns documented: heartbeat, reconnection, acknowledgment strategy
71	- [ ] Response codes are explicit (not "returns the todo" but "returns 201 with the created todo")
72	
73	### Security Checklist
74	- [ ] Trust boundaries identified (which inputs are untrusted?)
75	- [ ] Input validation specified for every endpoint/handler (schema, max size)
76	- [ ] Rate limiting specified for expensive operations (AI API calls, file uploads)
77	- [ ] Authentication/authorization requirements documented
78	- [ ] Secrets management documented (.env pattern, .gitignore verification)
79	
80	### UI/UX Checklist
81	If the plan includes a user interface:
82	- [ ] User flows documented with trigger, steps, and visible outcome (see UI-FLOWS.md template)
83	- [ ] Text-based wireframes for each screen showing layout and interactive elements
84	- [ ] Empty states, loading states, and error states defined for each view
85	- [ ] Integration work units explicitly created to wire components into the app shell
86	- [ ] Component hierarchy documented (what renders what, where)
87	
88	### External Dependencies Checklist
89	- [ ] All external services identified (APIs, SDKs, third-party services)
90	- [ ] Required credentials/config documented (env var names, how to obtain)
91	- [ ] Human checkpoint planned BEFORE work units that depend on external services
92	- [ ] Graceful degradation specified for when credentials are missing
93	- [ ] `.env.example` includes all required env vars
94	
95	### Completeness Checklist
96	- [ ] All human checkpoints from the spec are included
97	- [ ] All features from the spec have at least one work unit
98	- [ ] Tooling is consistent (one package manager, matching config files across WUs)
99	- [ ] No WU exceeds ~5 files or ~3 distinct concerns
100	- [ ] WUs that are too large are split with explicit dependencies between parts
101	
102	**If any checklist item fails, fix the plan BEFORE submitting to Design Review Gate.**
103	
104	### Required Plan Sections
105	
106	Every plan submitted for Design Review MUST include these sections:
107	
108	1. **Work Unit Decomposition** вҖ” WU list with DoD items, file scopes, dependencies
109	2. **API Contract** (if applicable) вҖ” structured endpoint/protocol specs:
110	   ```markdown
111	   ### POST /api/todos
112	   - **Request Body**: `{ title: string }` (required, 1-500 chars, trimmed)
113	   - **Success**: `201 Created` -> `{ id, title, completed, createdAt, updatedAt }`
114	   - **Errors**: `400` (validation) / `500` (internal)
115	   ```
116	3. **Security Considerations** вҖ” trust boundaries, input validation table, rate limiting table, secrets management
117	4. **User Flows** (if UI exists) вҖ” text wireframes and interaction flows (use UI-FLOWS.md template)
118	5. **External Dependencies** вҖ” services, credentials, setup instructions
119	6. **Human Checkpoints** вҖ” named pause points with review criteria
120	
121	---
122	
123	## 2. Work Unit Decomposition
124	
125	A **work unit** is the atomic unit of orchestrated execution. Before entering the 4-phase loop, decompose the implementation plan into work units.
126	
127	### Work Unit Structure
128	
129	Each work unit contains:
130	
131	| Field | Description | Example |
132	| --- | --- | --- |
133	| **ID** | Unique identifier (BEADS task ID) | `bd-wu-001` |
134	| **Title** | Human-readable name | "Implement auth middleware" |
135	| **Spec** | Written specification with acceptance criteria | Link to design doc section |
136	| **DoD Items** | Enumerated, verifiable done criteria | `[ ] Middleware rejects expired tokens` |
137	| **Dependencies** | Other work units that must complete first | `[bd-wu-000]` |
138	| **File Scope** | Files this work unit may touch | `src/middleware/auth.ts, src/middleware/auth.test.ts` |
139	| **Human Checkpoint** | Whether to pause for human review after completion | `true` for risky changes |
140	
141	### Constructing Dependency Graphs
142	
143	Work units form a directed acyclic graph (DAG):
144	
145	```text
146	wu-001 (schema changes) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
147	                            в”ңв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ wu-003 (API endpoints)  в”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ wu-005 (integration tests)
148	wu-002 (shared utilities) в”Җв”Җв”ҳ                                        в”Ӯ
149	                                                                     в–ј
150	wu-004 (UI components)  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ wu-006 (e2e tests)
151	```
152	
153	**Rules for decomposition:**
154	
155	1. Each work unit has a **single responsibility** вҖ” one logical change
156	2. File scopes should **not overlap** between parallel work units
157	3. Dependencies must be **explicit** вҖ” no implicit ordering assumptions
158	4. Work units at the same depth with no interdependencies **run in parallel**
159	5. Each DoD item must be **independently verifiable** (not "code looks good")
160	
161	### Decomposition Template
162	
163	```bash
164	# Create work units as BEADS tasks under the epic
165	bd create "WU-001: <title>" --type task --parent <epic-id> \
166	  --description "Spec: <spec-section>\nDoD:\n- [ ] <item-1>\n- [ ] <item-2>\nFile scope: <files>\nCheckpoint: <yes/no>"
167	
168	# Set up dependencies
169	bd dep add <wu-003> <wu-001>
170	bd dep add <wu-003> <wu-002>
171	```
172	
173	---
174	
175	## 3. The 4-Phase Execution Loop
176	
177	For each work unit, execute these four phases in sequence. **Do not skip phases.** Do not combine phases. Do not proceed to the next phase until the current phase produces a clear outcome.
178	
179	```text
180	в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
181	в”Ӯ                    4-PHASE EXECUTION LOOP                       в”Ӯ
182	в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
183	в”Ӯ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
184	в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ IMPLEMENTв”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’в”Ӯ VALIDATE в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’в”Ӯ  ADVERSARIAL в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’в”ӮCOMMITв”Ӯ в”Ӯ
185	в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ    REVIEW     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
186	в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ
187	в”Ӯ        в–І                                 в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ
188	в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ              FAIL               в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ
189	в”Ӯ        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                      в”Ӯ
190	в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
191	в”Ӯ   On FAIL: fix вҶ’ re-validate вҶ’ FRESH review вҶ’ max 3 вҶ’ escalate в”Ӯ
192	в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
193	```
194	
195	### Phase 1: IMPLEMENT
196	
197	The coding subagent executes against the work unit spec.
198	
199	**Orchestrator actions:**
200	
201	1. Spawn a coding subagent with the work unit spec, DoD items, file scope, and the **Project Context Document**
202	2. The subagent implements the change following TDD (test first, then implementation)
203	3. The subagent reports completion вҖ” **but the orchestrator does NOT trust this report**
204	
205	**Subagent spawn template:**
206	
207	```text
208	You are the CODER AGENT for work unit ${wuId}.
209	
210	## Spec
211	${spec}
212	
213	## Definition of Done
214	${dodItems.map((item, i) => `${i+1}. ${item}`).join('\n')}
215	
216	## File Scope
217	You may ONLY modify these files: ${fileScope.join(', ')}
218	
219	## Project Context
220	${projectContext}
221	
222	## Rules
223	- Follow TDD: write failing test first, then implement to make it pass
224	- Do NOT modify files outside your file scope
225	- Do NOT self-certify вҖ” the orchestrator will validate independently
226	- When complete, report what you changed and what tests you added
227	- NEVER use --no-verify on git commits вҖ” pre-commit hooks are mandatory
228	- NEVER use git push --force
229	- NEVER suppress linter/type errors with eslint-disable, @ts-ignore, or as any
230	- NEVER skip tests or claim "tests pass" without actually running them
231	```
232	
233	**Phase 1 output:** List of changed files and new tests.
234	
235	### Phase 2: VALIDATE
236	
237	The **orchestrator independently** runs quality gates. Never trust subagent self-reports.
238	
239	**Orchestrator actions (run these yourself, NOT via the coding subagent):**
240	
241	```bash
242	# 1. Type checking
243	npx tsc --noEmit
244	
245	# 2. Linting
246	npx eslint <changed-files>
247	
248	# 3. Run tests (full suite, not just new tests)
249	npx vitest run
250	
251	# 4. Coverage enforcement (BLOCKING вҖ” read .coverage-thresholds.json)
252	# If .coverage-thresholds.json exists, read the enforcement command and run it
253	# This is NOT optional. Coverage below threshold = VALIDATION FAIL.
254	if [ -f .coverage-thresholds.json ]; then
255	  CMD=$(node -e "console.log(JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('.coverage-thresholds.json','utf-8')).enforcement.command)")
256	  eval "$CMD"
257	fi
258	
259	# 5. Verify file scope was respected
260	git diff --name-only | while read file; do
261	  echo "$file" # Check each file is within the work unit's declared scope
262	done
263	```
264	
265	**Phase 2 outcomes:**
266	
267	- **All gates pass** вҶ’ proceed to Phase 3
268	- **Any gate fails** вҶ’ return to Phase 1 (the coding subagent fixes the issue)
269	- **File scope violated** вҶ’ return to Phase 1 (subagent must revert out-of-scope changes)
270	
271	**Critical rule:** The orchestrator runs validation commands directly. The orchestrator does NOT ask the coding subagent "did the tests pass?" and accept the answer.
272	
273	### Phase 3: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW
274	
275	A **separate review subagent** checks the implementation against the spec contract. This is NOT the same as a collaborative code review вҖ” it's adversarial verification.
276	
277	**Key differences from collaborative review:**
278	
279	| Collaborative Review | Adversarial Review |
280	| --- | --- |
281	| APPROVED / CHANGES REQUIRED | PASS / FAIL |
282	| Subjective quality assessment | Binary spec compliance check |
283	| Reviewer suggests improvements | Reviewer finds contract violations |
284	| Same reviewer can re-review | Fresh reviewer required on re-review |
285	| Uses `code-review-rubric.md` | Uses `adversarial-review-rubric.md` |
286	
287	**Orchestrator actions:**
288	
289	1. Spawn a **new** review subagent in adversarial mode
290	2. Pass: the spec, the DoD items, and the diff (NOT the coding subagent's self-assessment)
291	3. The reviewer checks each DoD item with evidence (file:line references)
292	
293	**Reviewer spawn template:**
294	
295	```text
296	You are the ADVERSARIAL REVIEWER for work unit ${wuId}.
297	
298	## Mode
299	Adversarial вҖ” your job is to FIND FAILURES, not to approve.
300	
301	## Rubric
302	Read and follow: ./rubrics/adversarial-review-rubric.md
303	
304	## Spec
305	${spec}
306	
307	## Definition of Done
308	${dodItems.map((item, i) => `${i+1}. ${item}`).join('\n')}
309	
310	## What to Review
311	Run: git diff main..HEAD -- ${fileScope.join(' ')}
312	
313	## Rules
314	- Check EACH DoD item. Cite file:line evidence for PASS or expected-vs-found for FAIL.
315	- Any single BLOCKING issue means overall FAIL.
316	- You have NO context from previous reviews. Judge fresh.
317	- Do NOT suggest improvements. Only report PASS or FAIL with evidence.
318	```
319	
320	**Phase 3 outcomes:**
321	
322	- **PASS** (zero BLOCKING issues) вҶ’ proceed to Phase 4
323	- **FAIL** (any BLOCKING issue) вҶ’ return to Phase 1 with the failure report
324	
325	**Fresh reviewer rule:** On re-review after FAIL, the orchestrator MUST spawn a **new** review subagent. Never pass previous findings to the new reviewer. Never reuse the same reviewer instance. This prevents anchoring bias and ensures independent verification.
326	
327	### Phase 4: COMMIT
328	
329	Only after PASS from adversarial review.
330	
331	**Orchestrator actions:**
332	
333	```bash
334	# Stage only files within the work unit's file scope
335	git add <file-scope-files>
336	
337	# Commit with reference to work unit
338	git commit -m "feat(wu-${wuId}): <description>
339	
340	DoD items verified:
341	$(dodItems.map((item, i) => `- [x] ${item}`).join('\n'))
342	
343	Reviewed-by: adversarial-review (PASS)"
344	```
345	
346	**After commit:**
347	- Update BEADS task status: `bd close <wu-task-id> --reason "4-phase loop complete. PASS."`
348	- If this work unit has a **human checkpoint** flag, pause and report before continuing
349	- Update the **Project Context Document** with completed work unit details
350	
351	**After commit, update SERVICE-INVENTORY.md:**
352	If this work unit created or modified services, factories, database tables, or shared modules, update `SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` with the new entries. This document is read by subsequent coder agents to avoid duplicating existing services.
353	
354	---
355	
356	## 4. Quality Gate Enforcement
357	
358	Quality gates are BLOCKING STATE TRANSITIONS, not advisory recommendations. The orchestrator CANNOT advance to the next phase without gate passage.
359	
360	### State Machine
361	
362	```text
363	IMPLEMENT в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ VALIDATE в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ REVIEW в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ COMMIT
364	                 в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
365	                 вҶ“            вҶ“
366	              FAIL:         FAIL:
367	           fix + re-run   fix + re-validate
368	                          + FRESH re-review
369	                 в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
370	              (max 3)      (max 3)
371	                 в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
372	                 вҶ“            вҶ“
373	              ESCALATE     ESCALATE
374	             (to human)   (to human)
375	```
376	
377	### Transition Rules (MUST, not SHOULD)
378	
379	1. **IMPLEMENT вҶ’ VALIDATE**: Always. No exceptions.
380	2. **VALIDATE вҶ’ REVIEW**: ONLY if ALL validation checks pass:
381	   - Tests pass (exit code 0)
382	   - Coverage meets `.coverage-thresholds.json` thresholds
383	   - Type checking passes
384	   - Lint passes
385	   - File scope respected
386	3. **REVIEW вҶ’ COMMIT**: ONLY if adversarial review returns PASS
387	4. **FAIL вҶ’ retry**: Fix the issue, then re-run the FAILED gate (not skip it)
388	5. **Re-review after fix**: MUST spawn a FRESH reviewer (new instance, no memory)
389	6. **Max retries**: 3 attempts per gate, then ESCALATE to human with full failure history
390	
391	### On FAIL: Mandatory Re-Review Protocol
392	
393	1. Fix the issue identified by the reviewer
394	2. Re-run Phase 2 (VALIDATE) вҖ” ALL quality gates, not just the one that failed
395	3. **MANDATORY**: Spawn a NEW adversarial reviewer (fresh instance, no memory of previous review)
396	4. Only COMMIT after the fresh reviewer returns PASS
397	5. Max 3 retry cycles before ESCALATE to human
398	
399	Track each attempt visibly: "Re-review attempt 1/3", "Re-review attempt 2/3", etc.
400	
401	### What the Orchestrator MUST NOT Do
402	
403	- "Coverage is close enough at 92%, proceeding to commit"
404	- "Adversarial review found issues but they're minor, committing anyway"
405	- "Fix applied, skipping re-review since the fix is straightforward"
406	- "5 FAILs encountered, moving to next work unit without resolution"
407	- "Tests pass but coverage command failed вҖ” proceeding anyway"
408	
409	### What the Orchestrator MUST Do
410	
411	- "VALIDATION FAIL: coverage at 87%, threshold is 100%. Returning to IMPLEMENT."
412	- "ADVERSARIAL REVIEW FAIL. Spawning fresh reviewer for re-review. Attempt 2 of 3."
413	- "Max retries (3) exceeded for WU-004. Escalating to human with failure history."
414	- "Fix applied. Re-running validation. Re-running adversarial review with FRESH reviewer."
415	
416	---
417	
418	## 5. Parallel Work Unit Execution
419	
420	When multiple work units have no dependencies on each other, execute them in parallel вҖ” but with structured convergence points.
421	
422	```text
423	              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-001: IMPLEMENT в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
424	              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
425	Fan-out в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-002: IMPLEMENT в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Converge for VALIDATE
426	              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
427	              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-003: IMPLEMENT в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
428	                                           в”Ӯ
429	              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-001: REVIEW в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
430	              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
431	Fan-out в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-002: REVIEW в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Sequential COMMIT
432	              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
433	              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-003: REVIEW в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
434	```
435	
436	**Rules for parallel execution:**
437	
438	1. **Fan-out implementations**: Spawn coding subagents for independent work units simultaneously
439	2. **Converge for validation**: Wait for ALL parallel implementations to complete, then validate each
440	3. **Fan-out reviews**: Spawn review subagents for each work unit simultaneously
441	4. **Sequential commits**: Commit work units one at a time to maintain clean git history
442	5. **If any FAIL**: Only re-run the failed work unit's loop вҖ” don't re-run passed units
443	
444	**Stale notification handling:** When parallel subagent results arrive after the orchestrator has moved past their work unit (e.g., at a checkpoint), acknowledge them briefly in one line. Do NOT print a full "still waiting at checkpoint" block for each stale notification вҖ” this clutters the conversation and wastes context window.
445	
446	---
447	
448	## 6. Project Context Document
449	
450	The orchestrator MUST maintain a project context document that grows with each work unit. This is passed to every coder subagent to prevent context loss.
451	
452	### Context Document Structure
453	
454	```markdown
455	# Project Context (Maintained by Orchestrator)
456	
457	## Tooling
458	- Package manager: <npm/pnpm/yarn>
459	- Test runner: <vitest/jest> (<config-file>)
460	- Linter: <eslint> (<config-file>)
461	- Build: <vite/webpack/tsc> (<config-file>)
462	
463	## Completed Work Units
464	| WU | Title | Key Files | Services Created |
465	|----|-------|-----------|-----------------|
466	
467	## Established Patterns
468	- <pattern-1>: <description>
469	- <pattern-2>: <description>
470	
471	## Active Services
472	See SERVICE-INVENTORY.md
473	```
474	
475	**Update rules:**
476	- After each Phase 4 (COMMIT), add the completed work unit to the table
477	- After each Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT), update patterns if new ones emerge
478	- Pass this document to every coder subagent alongside the work unit spec
479	
480	### Persisting to Disk (MANDATORY)
481	
482	The Project Context Document MUST be written to `.beads/context/project-context.md` and kept in sync with the in-memory version. This ensures the context survives context compaction and session boundaries.
483	
484	```bash
485	# Create directory if needed
486	mkdir -p .beads/context
487	
488	# Write/update after each Phase 4 (COMMIT) and at orchestration start
489	# The file should always reflect the current state of execution
490	```
491	
492	**When to write:**
493	- At orchestration start (initial creation)
494	- After each Phase 4 (COMMIT) вҖ” add the completed work unit
495	- After each Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT) вҖ” update patterns if new ones emerge
496	- At human checkpoints вҖ” snapshot current state
497	
498	**The file is NOT committed to git** during execution вҖ” it's a working document. It gets cleaned up after the PR is created (or left for the next session if interrupted).

502	## 6.5. Plan Persistence (Context Recovery)
503	
504	Approved plans and execution state are persisted to `.beads/` so agents can recover after context compaction or session interruption.
505	
506	### What Gets Persisted
507	
508	| File | Contents | Written When |
509	|------|----------|-------------|
510	| `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` | The adversarially-reviewed, user-approved implementation plan | After plan review gate PASS + user approval |
511	| `.beads/context/project-context.md` | Project Context Document (tooling, completed WUs, patterns) | After each Phase 4 COMMIT |
512	| `.beads/context/execution-state.md` | Current work unit, phase, retry count | After each phase transition |
513	
514	### Writing the Approved Plan
515	
516	After the Plan Review Gate approves a plan AND the user approves it, persist immediately:
517	
518	```bash
519	mkdir -p .beads/plans
520	
521	# Write the approved plan with metadata header
522	cat > .beads/plans/active-plan.md << 'PLAN_EOF'
523	# Active Plan
524	<!-- approved: <timestamp> -->
525	<!-- gate-iterations: <N> -->
526	<!-- user-approved: true -->
527	<!-- status: in-progress -->
528	
529	<full plan text including work unit decomposition, DoD items, file scopes, dependencies>
530	PLAN_EOF
531	```
532	
533	### Writing Execution State
534	
535	After each phase transition, update the execution state:
536	
537	```bash
538	cat > .beads/context/execution-state.md << 'STATE_EOF'
539	# Execution State
540	<!-- updated: <timestamp> -->
541	
542	## Current Position
543	- Active work unit: <wu-id>
544	- Current phase: <IMPLEMENT|VALIDATE|REVIEW|COMMIT>
545	- Retry count: <0-3>
546	
547	## Work Unit Status
548	| WU | Status | Phase | Retries |
549	|----|--------|-------|---------|
550	| WU-001 | COMPLETE | COMMITTED | 0 |
551	| WU-002 | IN-PROGRESS | VALIDATE | 1 |

556	STATE_EOF

559	### Context Recovery Protocol
560	
561	When the orchestrator detects it has lost context (after compaction or in a new session), it recovers by reading persisted state:
562	
563	```text
564	1. Check: Does `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` exist with `status: in-progress`?
565	   - YES вҶ’ Context was lost mid-execution. Recover.
566	   - NO вҶ’ No active execution. Start fresh.
567	
568	2. Recovery steps:
569	   a. Read `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` вҖ” reload the approved plan
570	   b. Read `.beads/context/project-context.md` вҖ” reload completed work and patterns
571	   c. Read `.beads/context/execution-state.md` вҖ” find where execution stopped
572	   d. Run `bd prime --work-type recovery` вҖ” reload relevant knowledge base facts
573	   e. Resume from the current work unit and phase
574	
575	3. Announce recovery to user:
576	   "Recovered execution context from BEADS. Resuming from WU-<id>, Phase <phase>."
577	```
578	
579	**When to trigger recovery:** The orchestrator should check for `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` at the start of any orchestrated execution. If the file exists with `status: in-progress` and the orchestrator has no plan in its current context, it's a recovery scenario.
580	
581	### Cleanup
582	
583	After the PR is created (or the plan is abandoned):
584	
585	```bash

588	  sed -i '' 's/status: in-progress/status: completed/' .beads/plans/active-plan.md
589	else
590	  sed -i 's/status: in-progress/status: completed/' .beads/plans/active-plan.md
591	fi
592	
593	# Archive execution state (don't delete вҖ” useful for post-mortem)
594	mv .beads/context/execution-state.md .beads/context/execution-state-<timestamp>.md
595	
596	# Project context can be kept for reference
597	```

601	## 7. Human Checkpoints (Proactive)
602	
603	Human checkpoints are **planned pauses**, not reactive escalations. They are defined in the spec before execution begins.
604	
605	### When to Set Checkpoints
606	
607	- After work units that change database schemas
608	- After work units that modify security-sensitive code
609	- After the first work unit in a new architectural pattern
610	- Before any destructive or irreversible operation
611	- At natural boundaries the human specified in the issue
612	- Before work units that depend on external services (APIs, SDKs requiring credentials)
613	  - Present: service name, required env vars, how to obtain credentials
614	  - Ask: "Do you have these configured? [Y/n]"
615	
616	### Checkpoint Report Format
617	
618	When reaching a checkpoint, present this report and **wait for explicit human approval**:
619	
620	```markdown
621	## Checkpoint: <checkpoint-name>
622	
623	### Completed Work Units
624	| WU | Title | Status | Review |
625	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
626	| WU-001 | Schema migration | PASS | Adversarial PASS |
627	| WU-002 | Service layer | PASS | Adversarial PASS |
628	
629	### Key Decisions Made
630	- <decision-1>: <rationale>
631	- <decision-2>: <rationale>
632	
633	Record significant decisions persistently with `bd decision "<decision>: <rationale>"` so they survive compaction and are available across sessions.
634	
635	### What Comes Next
636	- WU-003: <description>
637	- WU-004: <description>
638	
639	### Questions for Human (if any)
640	- <question>
641	
642	---
643	**Action required**: Reply to continue, or provide feedback to adjust course.
644	```
645	
646	**Do NOT continue past a checkpoint without human response.** This is not a notification вҖ” it's a gate.

650	## 8. Final Comprehensive Review
651	
652	After ALL work units are complete and committed, run a final comprehensive review across the entire change set. This catches cross-unit integration issues that per-unit reviews miss.
653	
654	### Final Review Checklist
655	
656	```bash
657	# 1. Combined diff вҖ” see the full picture
658	git diff main..HEAD
659	
660	# 2. Full test suite вҖ” not just changed files
661	npx vitest run
662	
663	# 3. Type check вҖ” catch cross-unit type conflicts
664	npx tsc --noEmit
665	
666	# 4. Lint вҖ” catch cross-unit style issues
667	npx eslint .
668	
669	# 5. Coverage вҖ” verify overall coverage thresholds
670	if [ -f .coverage-thresholds.json ]; then
671	  CMD=$(node -e "console.log(JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('.coverage-thresholds.json','utf-8')).enforcement.command)")
672	  eval "$CMD"
673	fi
674	
675	# 6. Commit history вҖ” verify clean, logical commits
676	git log main..HEAD --oneline
677	```
678	
679	### Cross-Unit Integration Checks
680	
681	- [ ] No duplicate or conflicting imports across work units
682	- [ ] No conflicting type definitions
683	- [ ] No overlapping test fixtures that could cause interference
684	- [ ] API contracts between work units are consistent
685	- [ ] No leftover TODO/FIXME markers from implementation
686	- [ ] File scope boundaries were respected (no unexpected file changes)
687	- [ ] SERVICE-INVENTORY.md is up to date with all created services
688	
689	### Final Report Format
690	
691	```markdown
692	## Final Comprehensive Review
693	
694	### Overall Verdict: PASS / FAIL
695	
696	### Work Units Summary
697	| WU | Title | Impl | Validate | Review | Commit |
698	| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
699	| WU-001 | <title> | Done | Pass | Pass | <sha> |
700	| WU-002 | <title> | Done | Pass | Pass | <sha> |
701	
702	### Quality Gates
703	- [ ] All tests pass
704	- [ ] Type check clean
705	- [ ] Lint clean
706	- [ ] Coverage thresholds met
707	- [ ] No cross-unit integration issues
708	
709	### Remaining Issues

711	
712	### Ready for PR: YES / NO
713	```

716	
717	## 8.5. Pre-PR Knowledge Capture (MANDATORY)
718	
719	After the final comprehensive review passes but BEFORE creating the PR, run `/self-reflect` to extract learnings into the knowledge base. This captures implementation insights, debugging discoveries, and architectural decisions while context is freshest вҖ” not deferred to post-merge when details have faded.
720	
721	```text
722	## Pre-PR Knowledge Capture
723	
724	Final review PASSED. Before creating the PR, extracting learnings...
725	
726	/self-reflect
727	
728	Learnings captured: [N] items added to knowledge base.
729	Committing knowledge base updates...
730	Proceeding to PR creation.
731	```
732	
733	**Why before PR, not after merge?** By the time a PR is merged, the implementing agent's context may be gone (session ended, context compacted). The richest insights вҖ” why a certain approach was chosen, what debugging dead-ends were hit, which patterns emerged вҖ” exist NOW, immediately after implementation. Capture them now.
734	
735	**Knowledge base changes are part of the PR.** After self-reflect updates the knowledge base files, commit them alongside the implementation. This ensures learnings are reviewed as part of the PR and land atomically with the code that generated them. Do NOT defer knowledge base commits to a separate PR or post-merge step.
736

738	
739	## 9. Recovery Protocol
740	
741	When things go wrong during the 4-phase loop, follow this structured recovery.
742	
743	### Step 1: DIAGNOSE
744	
745	Identify what failed and gather evidence:
746	
747	```bash
748	# Capture the failure
749	# - Which phase failed? (IMPLEMENT, VALIDATE, REVIEW)
750	# - What was the error message or FAIL reason?
751	# - Which DoD items are affected?
752	```
753	
754	### Step 2: CLASSIFY
755	
756	Categorize the failure:
757	
758	| Classification | Description | Action |
759	| --- | --- | --- |
760	| **Fixable** | Clear error, known fix | Retry with specific fix instructions |
761	| **Ambiguous** | Unclear root cause | Investigate before retrying |
762	| **External** | Dependency, access, or environment issue | Escalate immediately |
763	
764	### Step 3: RETRY (max 3 attempts)
765	
766	For fixable and ambiguous failures:
767	
768	1. **Attempt 1**: Fix the specific issue, re-run from Phase 1
769	2. **Attempt 2**: If same failure, try alternative approach
770	3. **Attempt 3**: If still failing, gather all evidence for escalation
771	
772	Track retry count:
773	
774	```bash
775	bd label add <task-id> retry:1  # or retry:2, retry:3
776	```
777	
778	### Step 4: ESCALATE
779	
780	After 3 failed attempts, escalate to human with full context:
781	
782	```markdown
783	## Escalation: Work Unit <wu-id> Failed After 3 Attempts
784	
785	### Failure History
786	| Attempt | Phase | Error | Fix Tried |
787	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
788	| 1 | VALIDATE | Tests fail: auth.test.ts:34 | Fixed mock setup |
789	| 2 | REVIEW | DoD #3 not met: missing edge case | Added edge case test |
790	| 3 | VALIDATE | Type error in cross-module import | Restructured imports |
791	
792	### Root Cause Assessment
793	<best understanding of why this keeps failing>
794	
795	### Options
796	1. <option-1>
797	2. <option-2>
798	3. Abandon this work unit and restructure
799	
800	### Recommendation
801	<which option and why>
802	```
803

805	
806	## 10. Anti-Patterns
807	
808	These are explicit DON'Ts. Violating any of these undermines the entire orchestration pattern.
809	
810	| # | Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | What to Do Instead |
811	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
812	| 1 | **Self-certifying** вҖ” coding subagent says "tests pass" and you believe it | Subagents can hallucinate, skip tests, or misinterpret results | Orchestrator runs validation commands independently |
813	| 2 | **Skipping adversarial review** вҖ” "the code looks fine, let's commit" | Visual inspection misses spec violations; confirmation bias | Always run adversarial review against DoD items |
814	| 3 | **Reusing a reviewer** вҖ” same subagent re-reviews after FAIL | Anchoring bias: reviewer remembers previous findings and checks for those specifically instead of reviewing fresh | Spawn a new reviewer instance with no prior context |
815	| 4 | **Passing previous findings to new reviewer** вҖ” "last reviewer found X, check if fixed" | Creates anchoring bias; new reviewer should find issues independently | Pass only: spec, DoD items, diff. Nothing about previous reviews |
816	| 5 | **Trusting subagent file scope claims** вҖ” "I only changed the files in scope" | Subagents may accidentally modify files outside scope | Run `git diff --name-only` and verify each file independently |
817	| 6 | **Combining phases** вҖ” "implement and validate in one step" | Removes the independence that makes validation meaningful | Run each phase as a distinct step with its own output |
818	| 7 | **Continuing past a checkpoint without human response** | Defeats the purpose of proactive checkpoints | Wait. If urgent, escalate вҖ” don't skip |
819	| 8 | **Skipping final comprehensive review** вҖ” "all units passed individually" | Per-unit reviews can't catch cross-unit integration issues | Always run the final review after all units are committed |
820	| 9 | **Skipping coverage enforcement** вҖ” "tests pass, coverage doesn't matter" | Coverage thresholds exist for a reason; low coverage means untested paths | Read .coverage-thresholds.json and run the enforcement command. Block on failure. |
821	| 10 | **Building UI components in isolation** вҖ” all components tested but never wired into the app | Users can't interact with components that aren't rendered | Plan must include integration WUs that wire components into the app shell |
822	| 11 | **Proceeding without external credentials** вҖ” building features that require API keys without verifying the user has them | Features will fail at runtime; user discovers this after 10+ commits | Checkpoint before external-service WUs to verify credentials are configured |
823	| 12 | **Advisory quality gates** вҖ” treating FAIL as a suggestion rather than a blocking transition | Undermines the entire trust model; equivalent to skipping the gate | Quality gates are state transitions. FAIL means retry or escalate, never skip. |
824	| 13 | **Using `--no-verify`** вҖ” bypassing pre-commit hooks on git commits | Pre-commit hooks catch lint errors, type errors, and formatting issues before they enter history | Never use `--no-verify`. Fix the underlying issue instead. |
825	| 14 | **Skipping design review gate after brainstorming** вҖ” going directly from brainstorming to writing-plans | Expensive implementation work begins on unreviewed designs | Always run the 5-agent design review gate between brainstorming and planning |
826	| 15 | **Skipping plan review gate** вҖ” presenting a plan to the user without adversarial review | Plans with feasibility gaps, missing requirements, or scope creep reach implementation | Always run the 3-reviewer plan review gate before presenting any plan |
827

11	
12	# Plan Review Gate
13	
14	**Core principle**: No plan reaches the user without surviving independent adversarial scrutiny.
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	This skill automatically activates after any implementation plan is drafted, before presenting it to the user. It spawns three independent adversarial reviewers in parallel вҖ” Feasibility, Completeness, and Scope & Alignment вҖ” each as a fresh `Task()` instance. ALL three must PASS. On failure, the planner incorporates feedback and resubmits to entirely fresh reviewer instances. The gate iterates until consensus or max iterations.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## Activation Triggers
23	
24	This skill auto-activates when:
25	
26	1. An implementation plan is drafted (by planner, explorer, or orchestrator)
27	2. The `writing-plans` skill completes a plan
28	3. The orchestrator's plan validation phase produces a plan for review
29	4. User explicitly requests: `/plan-review <path-to-plan>`
30	
31	**Do NOT use for**: Trivial changes (single-file bug fixes, copy edits, config tweaks). The plan must have at least 2 work units or touch 3+ files to warrant gate review.
32	
33	---
34	
35	## The 3 Adversarial Reviewers
36	
37	Each reviewer is a fresh `Task()` instance with read-only access to the codebase. Reviewers produce binary PASS/FAIL verdicts backed by cited evidence. No suggestions вҖ” only findings.
38	
39	### Reviewer 1: Feasibility
40	
41	Can this plan actually be executed against the real codebase?
42	
43	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
44	| --- | --- | --- |
45	| File paths exist | BLOCKING if fabricated | Every file path referenced in the plan must exist (verify with glob/grep) |
46	| Dependency ordering correct | BLOCKING if circular or forward-ref | Work units depend only on things that exist or are created before them |
47	| Technical approach matches codebase | BLOCKING if incompatible | Proposed patterns, libraries, and conventions match what the codebase actually uses |
48	| No unstated assumptions | BLOCKING if critical | Plan does not silently depend on services, env vars, or infrastructure that doesn't exist |
49	| File scope realistic | WARNING | Each work unit's file scope is achievable without cascading changes |
50	
51	### Reviewer 2: Completeness
52	
53	Does the plan fully address every aspect of the user's request?
54	
55	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
56	| --- | --- | --- |
57	| All requirements mapped | BLOCKING if gap exists | Every user requirement maps to at least one plan item |
58	| Verification steps defined | BLOCKING if missing | Each change has a way to verify it worked (test, manual check, or assertion) |
59	| Edge cases considered | BLOCKING if obvious gaps | Error scenarios, empty states, boundary conditions addressed |
60	| Rollback/backward compatibility | WARNING | Plan considers what happens if changes need to be reverted |
61	| Cross-file integration points | BLOCKING if missing | Files that import/depend on changed files are accounted for |
62	
63	### Reviewer 3: Scope & Alignment
64	
65	Is the plan right-sized for what the user actually asked?
66	
67	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
68	| --- | --- | --- |
69	| Matches user request | BLOCKING if divergent | Plan solves what was asked, not what the planner finds interesting |
70	| No scope creep | BLOCKING if present | No unnecessary features, abstractions, or refactoring beyond the request |
71	| No under-scoping | BLOCKING if present | Obvious implications of the request are not omitted |
72	| Complexity proportional | WARNING | Solution complexity matches problem complexity |
73	| No simpler alternative missed | WARNING | Plan is not over-engineered when a simpler approach would suffice |
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Reviewer Isolation Rules
78	
79	These rules are **mandatory**. Violating any of them invalidates the review.
80	
81	1. **Fresh instances only.** Each reviewer is a new `Task()` instance вҖ” never resumed, never given prior context.
82	2. **No cross-reviewer visibility.** No reviewer sees another reviewer's output. Ever.
83	3. **Read-only codebase access.** Reviewers can read files, run grep/glob, but cannot modify anything.
84	4. **No prior review findings on re-review.** When re-reviewing after iteration, completely fresh instances are spawned. The new reviewers have zero knowledge of what previous reviewers found.
85	5. **Input is limited to:** the plan text, the user's original request, and the codebase (via read-only tools).
86	
87	**Why isolation matters:** Reviewers who see prior findings anchor on those findings instead of reviewing independently. Fresh instances prevent this bias and ensure each review cycle is a genuine independent check.
88	
89	---
90	
91	## Workflow
92	
93	```text
94	1. Plan drafted by planner/explorer/orchestrator
95	2. Spawn 3 adversarial reviewers in PARALLEL (fresh Task() instances)
96	3. Collect all 3 verdicts
97	4. IF all PASS --> present plan to user with gate approval summary
98	5. IF any FAIL -->
99	   a. Planner reads ALL feedback from all reviewers
100	   b. Planner incorporates changes OR documents why feedback doesn't apply
101	   c. Spawn 3 NEW reviewer instances (never reuse -- prevents anchoring)
102	   d. Repeat from step 3
103	6. Max 3 iterations --> present plan with remaining issues noted for user decision
104	```
105	
106	### Phase 1: Spawn Reviewers (Parallel)
107	
108	```typescript
109	// Spawn all three reviewers in parallel for efficiency
110	const [feasibilityResult, completenessResult, scopeResult] = await Promise.all([
111	  Task({
112	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
113	    description: "Feasibility review",
114	    prompt: feasibilityReviewPrompt(planText, userRequest),
115	  }),
116	  Task({
117	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
118	    description: "Completeness review",
119	    prompt: completenessReviewPrompt(planText, userRequest),
120	  }),
121	  Task({
122	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
123	    description: "Scope & Alignment review",
124	    prompt: scopeAlignmentReviewPrompt(planText, userRequest),
125	  }),
126	]);
127	```
128	
129	### Phase 2: Evaluate Gate
130	
131	```text
132	IF feasibilityResult.verdict === "PASS"
133	   AND completenessResult.verdict === "PASS"
134	   AND scopeResult.verdict === "PASS":
135	  --> GATE APPROVED. Present plan to user.
136	
137	ELSE:
138	  --> Consolidate all FAIL findings.
139	  --> Pass to planner for revision.
140	  --> Increment iteration counter.
141	  --> IF iterations >= 3: present plan with remaining issues to user.
142	  --> ELSE: spawn fresh reviewers on revised plan.
143	```
144	
145	### Phase 3: Iteration (on FAIL)
146	
147	1. Planner receives consolidated feedback from ALL reviewers (both PASS and FAIL)
148	2. Planner revises the plan, addressing each FAIL finding explicitly
149	3. For each finding, planner either:
150	   - **Fixes it** вҖ” modifies the plan to resolve the issue
151	   - **Rebuts it** вҖ” documents why the finding is incorrect or not applicable (with evidence)
152	4. Spawn 3 entirely new `Task()` instances for re-review
153	5. New reviewers see ONLY the revised plan and original request вҖ” NOT previous findings
154	
155	### Phase 4: Escalation (after 3 iterations)
156	
157	If the gate has not achieved consensus after 3 iterations:
158	
159	```markdown
160	## Plan Review Gate: ESCALATION REQUIRED (3/3 iterations exhausted)
161	
162	### Remaining Blocking Issues
163	
164	#### Feasibility
165	- [issue with evidence]
166	
167	#### Completeness
168	- [issue with evidence]
169	
170	#### Scope & Alignment
171	- [issue with evidence]
172	
173	### Iteration History
174	| Iteration | Feasibility | Completeness | Scope & Alignment |
175	|-----------|-------------|--------------|-------------------|
176	| 1         | FAIL        | PASS         | FAIL              |
177	| 2         | PASS        | PASS         | FAIL              |
178	| 3         | PASS        | PASS         | FAIL              |
179	
180	### Options
181	1. **Override** вҖ” Proceed with plan as-is (remaining issues become known risks)
182	2. **Revise** вҖ” Continue iterating on the plan manually
183	3. **Simplify** вҖ” Reduce scope to eliminate contentious items
184	4. **Cancel** вҖ” Abandon this plan and start fresh
185	
186	Please choose an option or provide additional context.
187	```

367	## Anti-Patterns
368	
369	| # | Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | What to Do Instead |
370	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
371	| 1 | **Reusing reviewer instances** вҖ” passing findings to next review cycle | Anchoring bias: reviewer checks for previous findings instead of reviewing fresh | Spawn new `Task()` instances with no prior context |
372	| 2 | **Cross-reviewer contamination** вҖ” sharing one reviewer's output with another | Destroys independence; reviewers converge instead of checking independently | Each reviewer sees only: plan, user request, codebase |
373	| 3 | **Treating FAIL as advisory** вҖ” "reviewer failed but the issues are minor" | Undermines the gate; equivalent to not having a gate | FAIL means revise and re-review. No exceptions. |
374	| 4 | **Skipping the gate for "simple" plans** вҖ” "this is straightforward, skip review" | Judgment of simplicity is exactly what the gate validates | If the plan has 2+ work units or touches 3+ files, run the gate |
375	| 5 | **Planner self-reviewing** вҖ” same agent that wrote the plan also reviews it | Confirmation bias; planner will approve their own work | Reviewers must be separate `Task()` instances from the planner |
376	| 6 | **Unlimited iterations** вҖ” "keep reviewing until it passes" | Diminishing returns; likely a fundamental plan problem after 3 rounds | Max 3 iterations, then escalate to user with full context |
377	| 7 | **Partial re-review** вҖ” only re-running the reviewer that failed | Other reviewers might fail on the revised plan | All 3 reviewers re-run on every iteration (fresh instances) |

381	## Integration Points
382	
383	### Upstream (inputs to this gate)
384	
385	- `writing-plans` skill вҖ” produces plans that feed into this gate
386	- `orchestrated-execution` plan validation вҖ” pre-flight checklist runs before this gate
387	- Direct plan drafting by planner/explorer agents
388	
389	### Downstream (after gate approval)
390	
391	- Plan presented to user for final approval
392	- **After user approval**: Persist the approved plan to `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` (see Section: Plan Persistence below)
393	- User-approved plan flows to `orchestrated-execution` for the 4-phase execution loop
394	- Work unit decomposition and implementation begin
395	
396	### Plan Persistence
397	
398	After the gate approves AND the user approves the plan, persist it to BEADS so it survives context compaction:
399	
400	```bash
401	mkdir -p .beads/plans
402	
403	# Write with metadata header
404	cat > .beads/plans/active-plan.md << 'PLAN_EOF'
405	# Active Plan
406	<!-- approved: <timestamp> -->
407	<!-- gate-iterations: <N> -->
408	<!-- user-approved: true -->
409	<!-- status: in-progress -->
410	
411	<full approved plan text>
412	PLAN_EOF
413	```
414	
415	This enables context recovery вҖ” if the agent loses context mid-execution, it can re-read the approved plan from disk instead of re-running the entire gate. See `orchestrated-execution` Section 6.5 for the full recovery protocol.
416	
417	### Relationship to Design Review Gate
418	
419	The **design review gate** (`design-review-gate`) validates _design documents_ using 5 specialist agents (PM, Architect, Designer, Security, CTO) with APPROVED/NEEDS_REVISION verdicts.
420	
421	The **plan review gate** (this skill) validates _implementation plans_ using 3 adversarial reviewers (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) with binary PASS/FAIL verdicts.
422	
423	These are complementary gates at different stages:
424	1. Design review gate validates _what_ to build
425	2. Plan review gate validates _how_ to build it
426	3. Orchestrated execution validates _that_ it was built correctly

11	# Design Review Gate
12	
13	## Purpose
14	
15	This skill automatically activates after a design document is created (typically via `superpowers:brainstorming`). It ensures complex features receive proper review before implementation begins by spawning five specialist agents:
16	
17	1. **Product Manager Agent** - Use case validation and user benefit review
18	2. **Architect Agent** - Technical architecture review
19	3. **Designer Agent** - UX/API design quality review
20	4. **Security Design Agent** - Security threat modeling and protection review
21	5. **CTO Agent** - Codebase alignment and TDD readiness review
22	
23	All five must approve before proceeding to implementation.
24	
25	---
26	
27	## Coordination Mode Note
28	
29	This skill supports both coordination modes:
30	
31	- **Task Mode** (default): Spawn 5 parallel `Task()` subagents for each review round. Fresh instances per round.
32	- **Team Mode**: `TeamCreate("review-{design-doc-name}")`, spawn 5 reviewers as named teammates (`pm`, `architect`, `designer`, `security`, `cto`). Reviewers retain context through revision cycles вҖ” saves 5 cold starts per re-review round. After approval, send `shutdown_request` to all, then `TeamDelete`.
33	
34	In either mode, the review criteria, iteration protocol, and escalation rules are identical. See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for mode detection.
35	
36	---
37	
38	## Activation Triggers
39	
40	This skill auto-activates when:
41	
42	1. A design document is committed to `docs/plans/*-design.md`
43	2. The `superpowers:brainstorming` skill completes
44	3. User explicitly requests: `/review-design <path-to-design.md>`
45	
46	---
47	
48	## Workflow
49	
50	### Phase 1: Spawn Review Agents (Parallel)
51	
52	```typescript
53	// Spawn all five agents in parallel for efficiency
54	const [pmResult, architectResult, designerResult, securityResult, ctoResult] = await Promise.all([
55	  Task({
56	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
57	    description: "PM review",
58	    prompt: pmReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
59	  }),
60	  Task({
61	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
62	    description: "Architect review",
63	    prompt: architectReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
64	  }),
65	  Task({
66	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
67	    description: "Designer review",
68	    prompt: designerReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
69	  }),
70	  Task({
71	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
72	    description: "Security design review",
73	    prompt: securityDesignReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
74	  }),
75	  Task({
76	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
77	    description: "CTO review",
78	    prompt: ctoReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
79	  }),
80	]);
81	```
82	
83	### Phase 2: Aggregate Results
84	
85	Each agent returns a structured review:
86	
87	```typescript
88	interface ReviewResult {
89	  agent: "product-manager" | "architect" | "designer" | "security-design" | "cto";
90	  verdict: "APPROVED" | "NEEDS_REVISION";
91	  blockers: string[]; // MUST fix before implementation
92	  suggestions: string[]; // Nice to have
93	  questions: string[]; // Clarifications needed
94	  use_case_analysis?: {
95	    // PM agent only
96	    total_use_cases: number;
97	    clear: number;
98	    needs_work: number;
99	    missing_scenarios: string[];
100	  };
101	  threat_model?: {
102	    // Security agent only
103	    high_risk: string[];
104	    medium_risk: string[];
105	    mitigations_required: string[];
106	  };
107	}
108	```
109	
110	### Phase 3: Check Gate
111	
112	```
113	IF all five agents return APPROVED:
114	  вҶ’ Proceed to implementation
115	  вҶ’ Create epic with approved design
116	
117	ELSE:
118	  вҶ’ Consolidate feedback
119	  вҶ’ Present to user
120	  вҶ’ Iterate on design
121	  вҶ’ Re-run gate (max 3 iterations)
122	```
123	
124	### Phase 4: Human Escalation
125	
126	After 3 failed iterations:
127	
128	1. Create summary of remaining blockers
129	2. Ask human to decide: override, defer, or cancel

1	# Swarm Coordinator Agent
2	
3	## Role
4	
5	Meta-orchestrator for the BEADS multi-agent swarm. Manages multiple GitHub Issues/BEADS epics in parallel, coordinates agent assignments, detects conflicts, and ensures efficient resource utilization across worktrees.
6	
7	## Responsibilities
8	
9	1. **Multi-Issue Orchestration**
10	   - Track all active GitHub Issues with `agent-ready` label
11	   - Spawn and coordinate Issue Orchestrator agents
12	   - Manage epic lifecycle across multiple parallel work streams
13	
14	2. **Load Balancing**
15	   - Monitor worktree utilization
16	   - Distribute work evenly across available worktrees
17	   - Prevent resource contention and overload
18	
19	3. **Conflict Detection**
20	   - Detect file-level conflicts (multiple agents touching same files)
21	   - Identify dependency conflicts between epics
22	   - Flag schema/migration conflicts before they occur
23	
24	4. **Prioritization**
25	   - Enforce priority ordering (P0 > P1 > P2 > P3 > P4)
26	   - Handle urgent escalations
27	   - Manage blocking dependencies across epics
28	
29	5. **Health Monitoring**
30	   - Track agent heartbeats
31	   - Detect stuck or failed agents
32	   - Trigger recovery procedures
33	
34	## Decision Authority
35	
36	The Swarm Coordinator can autonomously:
37	
38	- Assign Issues to worktrees
39	- Spawn Issue Orchestrator agents
40	- Rebalance work across worktrees
41	- Pause lower-priority work for P0/P1 issues
42	
43	The Swarm Coordinator must escalate:
44	
45	- Resource exhaustion (all worktrees full)
46	- Unresolvable conflicts
47	- Agent failures requiring human intervention
48	- Priority disputes
49	
50	## Coordination Mode
51	
52	At workflow start, check which coordination tools are available:
53	
54	```
55	IF TeamCreate AND SendMessage available вҶ’ Team Mode
56	ELSE вҶ’ Task Mode (default, current behavior)
57	```
58	
59	**Single check at start. Do not switch modes mid-workflow.**
60	
61	### Task Mode (Default)
62	
63	Fire-and-forget `Task()` subagents per worktree orchestrator. Each gets full context in its prompt. No cross-agent communication. This is the existing behavior.
64	
65	### Team Mode
66	
67	When Team tools are available:
68	
69	1. **Create initiative team**: `TeamCreate("swarm-{initiative-id}")` (e.g., `swarm-auth-overhaul`)
70	2. **Spawn Issue Orchestrators as named teammates**: `orch-123`, `orch-456`, etc.
71	3. **Broadcast conflicts**: Use `SendMessage(type: "broadcast")` to alert all orchestrators of file/schema conflicts
72	4. **Receive async updates**: Orchestrators send "PR merged", "blocked", "escalation" messages
73	5. **Enforce phase gates**: Via messaging (next phase starts only after all previous layer PRs merged)
74	6. **Graceful shutdown**: Send `shutdown_request` to each teammate, then `TeamDelete`
75	
76	**MANDATORY**: Adversarial reviewers are ALWAYS fresh `Task()` instances вҖ” never teammates, never resumed. See `guides/agent-coordination.md`.

108	
109	### Step 0: Knowledge Priming (CRITICAL)

121	## Coordination Protocol
122	
123	### Issue Intake
124	
125	```
126	1. GitHub webhook: Issue labeled 'agent-ready'
127	2. Swarm Coordinator receives event
128	3. Check priority and existing workload
129	4. Select available worktree OR queue if all busy
130	5. Spawn Issue Orchestrator in selected worktree
131	6. Record assignment in active-assignments.jsonl
132	7. Post GitHub comment: "рҹӨ– Agent claiming this work"
133	```
134	
135	### Conflict Resolution
136	
137	```
138	File Conflicts:
139	1. Detect overlapping file modifications
140	2. Check if changes are additive (can merge) or conflicting
141	3. If additive: Allow parallel execution
142	4. If conflicting: Sequence by priority, notify lower-priority agent to wait
143	
144	Schema Conflicts:
145	1. Detect migration conflicts
146	2. ALWAYS sequence schema changes
147	3. Higher priority goes first
148	4. Block lower priority until migration complete
149	```
150	
151	### Rebalancing
152	
153	```
154	Trigger: Worktree becomes idle OR high-priority issue arrives
155	1. Scan pending issues queue
156	2. Sort by: priority DESC, created_at ASC
157	3. Assign highest priority pending to idle worktree
158	4. If P0 arrives and all busy:
159	   - Pause lowest priority P3/P4 work
160	   - Assign P0 to freed worktree
161	   - Resume paused work when P0 completes
162	```

218	## Metrics
219	
220	The Swarm Coordinator tracks:
221	
222	- **Throughput**: Issues completed per day/week
223	- **Lead Time**: Time from `agent-ready` to PR merged
224	- **Cycle Time**: Time from work started to PR merged
225	- **Utilization**: % time worktrees are actively working
226	- **Conflict Rate**: Conflicts detected per issue

229	## Error Recovery
230	
231	### Agent Unresponsive
232	
233	```
234	1. Heartbeat timeout (5 minutes)
235	2. Send SIGTERM to orchestrator process
236	3. Wait 30 seconds
237	4. If still running: SIGKILL
238	5. Mark worktree as 'recovering'
239	6. Clean up worktree state
240	7. Reassign issue to fresh worktree
241	8. Post incident to #dev-alerts
242	```
243	
244	### Worktree Corruption
245	
246	```
247	1. Detect git errors or inconsistent state
248	2. Preserve logs and state for debugging
249	3. Delete and recreate worktree
250	4. Restart issue from last checkpoint
251	5. Notify human if data loss possible
252	```

254	## Configuration

274	## Recursive Swarm Orchestration
275	
276	The Swarm Coordinator can coordinate swarms of swarms for large initiatives:
277	
278	```text
279	Swarm Coordinator (top-level)
280	в”ңв”Җв”Җ Research Swarm вҶ’ use cases, competitor analysis, user interviews
281	в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Researcher Agent (domain A)
282	в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ Researcher Agent (domain B)
283	в”ңв”Җв”Җ Spec Swarm вҶ’ one spec per epic
284	в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Architect Agent (epic 1)
285	в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ Architect Agent (epic 2)
286	в””в”Җв”Җ Implementation Swarm вҶ’ one orchestrator per story
287	    в”ңв”Җв”Җ Issue Orchestrator (story 1) вҶ’ Sub-Orchestrators if needed

290	
291	**Pattern**: Decompose large initiatives into phases. Each phase is a swarm. Each swarm can recursively contain sub-swarms or individual orchestrators.

The orchestrator must verify `gh pr view <number> --json state -q .state` returns `MERGED` for every PR in the previous phase before launching agents for the next phase. ## File 1: orchestrated-execution/SKILL.md

```markdown

name: orchestrated-execution

# Orchestrated Execution Skill

**Core principle**: Trust nothing. Verify everything. Review adversarially.

This skill defines a generalized 4-phase execution loop that any orchestrator can invoke when implementing work units. It replaces linear "implement then review" flows with a rigorous cycle that independently validates results and adversarially reviews against a written spec contract.

---

## Coordination Mode Note

This skill is mode-agnostic вҖ” the 4-phase execution loop works identically in both Task Mode and Team Mode. The differences:

- **Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT)**: In Team Mode, the coding subagent may be a persistent teammate (retains context across work units). In Task Mode, it's a fresh `Task()` per work unit.
- **Phase 3 (ADVERSARIAL REVIEW)**: ALWAYS a fresh `Task()` instance in BOTH modes. Never a teammate, never resumed.
- **All quality gates**: Unchanged regardless of mode.

See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for full mode detection and coordination details.

---

## Plan Review Gate

After drafting an implementation plan (Step 1: Plan Validation), submit it to the **Plan Review Gate** before presenting to the user. The gate spawns 3 adversarial reviewers (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) вҖ” all must PASS. See `skills/plan-review-gate/SKILL.md` for details.

---

## When to Use This Skill

- **Complex tasks** decomposed into multiple work units
- **Tasks with a written spec** containing Definition of Done (DoD) items
- **Multi-agent orchestration** where subagents produce work that needs verification
- **High-stakes changes** where self-reported "it works" is insufficient

**Do NOT use for**: Single-file bug fixes, copy changes, or tasks without a spec.

---

## 1. Plan Validation (Pre-Flight Checklist)

Before submitting a plan to the Design Review Gate, the orchestrator MUST verify every item on this checklist. This prevents expensive design review cycles on fundamentally broken plans.

> **Note**: The `Plan` subagent type cannot write files (it has read-only access by design). If you spawn an Architect as a Plan subagent, it will return the plan as text in its response. The orchestrator must write the plan to `PLAN.md` itself.

### Architecture Checklist
- [ ] Every data access goes through a service layer (no direct DB calls from routes/handlers)
- [ ] Each work unit has a single responsibility (max ~5 files created/modified)
- [ ] Error handling strategy specified (typed error hierarchy, how errors cross layer boundaries)
- [ ] No hard-coded configuration вҖ” environment variables for all external service config

### Dependency Graph Checklist
- [ ] Each WU's dependencies are minimal (only depends on what it actually imports/uses)
- [ ] No unnecessary serialization вҖ” WUs that CAN be parallel ARE marked parallel
- [ ] No circular dependencies
- [ ] Integration WUs exist to wire components into the app shell (not just built in isolation)

### API Contract Checklist
If the plan includes HTTP endpoints or WebSocket protocols, verify:
- [ ] Every HTTP endpoint specifies: method, path, request schema, all response status codes, error response shapes
- [ ] WebSocket message types fully specified (client-to-server AND server-to-client message type tables)
- [ ] Protocol concerns documented: heartbeat, reconnection, acknowledgment strategy
- [ ] Response codes are explicit (not "returns the todo" but "returns 201 with the created todo")

### Security Checklist
- [ ] Trust boundaries identified (which inputs are untrusted?)
- [ ] Input validation specified for every endpoint/handler (schema, max size)
- [ ] Rate limiting specified for expensive operations (AI API calls, file uploads)
- [ ] Authentication/authorization requirements documented
- [ ] Secrets management documented (.env pattern, .gitignore verification)

### UI/UX Checklist
If the plan includes a user interface:
- [ ] User flows documented with trigger, steps, and visible outcome (see UI-FLOWS.md template)
- [ ] Text-based wireframes for each screen showing layout and interactive elements
- [ ] Empty states, loading states, and error states defined for each view
- [ ] Integration work units explicitly created to wire components into the app shell
- [ ] Component hierarchy documented (what renders what, where)

### External Dependencies Checklist
- [ ] All external services identified (APIs, SDKs, third-party services)
- [ ] Required credentials/config documented (env var names, how to obtain)
- [ ] Human checkpoint planned BEFORE work units that depend on external services
- [ ] Graceful degradation specified for when credentials are missing
- [ ] `.env.example` includes all required env vars

### Completeness Checklist
- [ ] All human checkpoints from the spec are included
- [ ] All features from the spec have at least one work unit
- [ ] Tooling is consistent (one package manager, matching config files across WUs)
- [ ] No WU exceeds ~5 files or ~3 distinct concerns
- [ ] WUs that are too large are split with explicit dependencies between parts

**If any checklist item fails, fix the plan BEFORE submitting to Design Review Gate.**

### Required Plan Sections

Every plan submitted for Design Review MUST include these sections:

1. **Work Unit Decomposition** вҖ” WU list with DoD items, file scopes, dependencies
2. **API Contract** (if applicable) вҖ” structured endpoint/protocol specs:
   ```markdown
   ### POST /api/todos
   - **Request Body**: `{ title: string }` (required, 1-500 chars, trimmed)
   - **Success**: `201 Created` -> `{ id, title, completed, createdAt, updatedAt }`
   - **Errors**: `400` (validation) / `500` (internal)
   ```
3. **Security Considerations** вҖ” trust boundaries, input validation table, rate limiting table, secrets management
4. **User Flows** (if UI exists) вҖ” text wireframes and interaction flows (use UI-FLOWS.md template)
5. **External Dependencies** вҖ” services, credentials, setup instructions
6. **Human Checkpoints** вҖ” named pause points with review criteria

## 2. Work Unit Decomposition

A **work unit** is the atomic unit of orchestrated execution. Before entering the 4-phase loop, decompose the implementation plan into work units.

### Work Unit Structure

Each work unit contains:

| Field | Description | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **ID** | Unique identifier (BEADS task ID) | `bd-wu-001` |
| **Title** | Human-readable name | "Implement auth middleware" |
| **Spec** | Written specification with acceptance criteria | Link to design doc section |
| **DoD Items** | Enumerated, verifiable done criteria | `[ ] Middleware rejects expired tokens` |
| **Dependencies** | Other work units that must complete first | `[bd-wu-000]` |
| **File Scope** | Files this work unit may touch | `src/middleware/auth.ts, src/middleware/auth.test.ts` |
| **Human Checkpoint** | Whether to pause for human review after completion | `true` for risky changes |

### Constructing Dependency Graphs

Work units form a directed acyclic graph (DAG):

```text
wu-001 (schema changes) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                            в”ңв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ wu-003 (API endpoints)  в”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ wu-005 (integration tests)
wu-002 (shared utilities) в”Җв”Җв”ҳ                                        в”Ӯ
                                                                     в–ј
wu-004 (UI components)  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ wu-006 (e2e tests)
```

**Rules for decomposition:**

1. Each work unit has a **single responsibility** вҖ” one logical change
2. File scopes should **not overlap** between parallel work units
3. Dependencies must be **explicit** вҖ” no implicit ordering assumptions
4. Work units at the same depth with no interdependencies **run in parallel**
5. Each DoD item must be **independently verifiable** (not "code looks good")

### Decomposition Template

```bash
# Create work units as BEADS tasks under the epic
bd create "WU-001: <title>" --type task --parent <epic-id> \
  --description "Spec: <spec-section>\nDoD:\n- [ ] <item-1>\n- [ ] <item-2>\nFile scope: <files>\nCheckpoint: <yes/no>"

# Set up dependencies
bd dep add <wu-003> <wu-001>
bd dep add <wu-003> <wu-002>
```

## 3. The 4-Phase Execution Loop

For each work unit, execute these four phases in sequence. **Do not skip phases.** Do not combine phases. Do not proceed to the next phase until the current phase produces a clear outcome.

```text
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                    4-PHASE EXECUTION LOOP                       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ    REVIEW     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ

```

### Phase 1: IMPLEMENT

The coding subagent executes against the work unit spec.

**Orchestrator actions:**

1. Spawn a coding subagent with the work unit spec, DoD items, file scope, and the **Project Context Document**
2. The subagent implements the change following TDD (test first, then implementation)
3. The subagent reports completion вҖ” **but the orchestrator does NOT trust this report**

**Subagent spawn template:**

```text
You are the CODER AGENT for work unit ${wuId}.

## Spec
${spec}

## Definition of Done
${dodItems.map((item, i) => `${i+1}. ${item}`).join('\n')}

## File Scope
You may ONLY modify these files: ${fileScope.join(', ')}

## Project Context
${projectContext}

## Rules
- Follow TDD: write failing test first, then implement to make it pass
- Do NOT modify files outside your file scope
- Do NOT self-certify вҖ” the orchestrator will validate independently
- When complete, report what you changed and what tests you added
- NEVER use --no-verify on git commits вҖ” pre-commit hooks are mandatory
- NEVER use git push --force
- NEVER suppress linter/type errors with eslint-disable, @ts-ignore, or as any
- NEVER skip tests or claim "tests pass" without actually running them
```

**Phase 1 output:** List of changed files and new tests.

### Phase 2: VALIDATE

The **orchestrator independently** runs quality gates. Never trust subagent self-reports.

**Orchestrator actions (run these yourself, NOT via the coding subagent):**

```bash
# 1. Type checking
npx tsc --noEmit

# 2. Linting
npx eslint <changed-files>

# 3. Run tests (full suite, not just new tests)
npx vitest run

# 4. Coverage enforcement (BLOCKING вҖ” read .coverage-thresholds.json)
# If .coverage-thresholds.json exists, read the enforcement command and run it
# This is NOT optional. Coverage below threshold = VALIDATION FAIL.
if [ -f .coverage-thresholds.json ]; then
  CMD=$(node -e "console.log(JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('.coverage-thresholds.json','utf-8')).enforcement.command)")
  eval "$CMD"
fi

# 5. Verify file scope was respected
git diff --name-only | while read file; do
  echo "$file" # Check each file is within the work unit's declared scope
done
```

**Phase 2 outcomes:**

- **All gates pass** вҶ’ proceed to Phase 3
- **Any gate fails** вҶ’ return to Phase 1 (the coding subagent fixes the issue)
- **File scope violated** вҶ’ return to Phase 1 (subagent must revert out-of-scope changes)

**Critical rule:** The orchestrator runs validation commands directly. The orchestrator does NOT ask the coding subagent "did the tests pass?" and accept the answer.

### Phase 3: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW

A **separate review subagent** checks the implementation against the spec contract. This is NOT the same as a collaborative code review вҖ” it's adversarial verification.

**Key differences from collaborative review:**

| Collaborative Review | Adversarial Review |
| --- | --- |
| APPROVED / CHANGES REQUIRED | PASS / FAIL |
| Subjective quality assessment | Binary spec compliance check |
| Reviewer suggests improvements | Reviewer finds contract violations |
| Same reviewer can re-review | Fresh reviewer required on re-review |
| Uses `code-review-rubric.md` | Uses `adversarial-review-rubric.md` |

**Orchestrator actions:**

1. Spawn a **new** review subagent in adversarial mode
2. Pass: the spec, the DoD items, and the diff (NOT the coding subagent's self-assessment)
3. The reviewer checks each DoD item with evidence (file:line references)

**Reviewer spawn template:**

```text
You are the ADVERSARIAL REVIEWER for work unit ${wuId}.

## Mode
Adversarial вҖ” your job is to FIND FAILURES, not to approve.

## Rubric
Read and follow: ./rubrics/adversarial-review-rubric.md

## Spec
${spec}

## Definition of Done
${dodItems.map((item, i) => `${i+1}. ${item}`).join('\n')}

## What to Review
Run: git diff main..HEAD -- ${fileScope.join(' ')}

## Rules
- Check EACH DoD item. Cite file:line evidence for PASS or expected-vs-found for FAIL.
- Any single BLOCKING issue means overall FAIL.
- You have NO context from previous reviews. Judge fresh.
- Do NOT suggest improvements. Only report PASS or FAIL with evidence.
```

**Phase 3 outcomes:**

- **PASS** (zero BLOCKING issues) вҶ’ proceed to Phase 4
- **FAIL** (any BLOCKING issue) вҶ’ return to Phase 1 with the failure report

**Fresh reviewer rule:** On re-review after FAIL, the orchestrator MUST spawn a **new** review subagent. Never pass previous findings to the new reviewer. Never reuse the same reviewer instance. This prevents anchoring bias and ensures independent verification.

### Phase 4: COMMIT

Only after PASS from adversarial review.

**Orchestrator actions:**

```bash
# Stage only files within the work unit's file scope
git add <file-scope-files>

# Commit with reference to work unit
git commit -m "feat(wu-${wuId}): <description>

DoD items verified:
$(dodItems.map((item, i) => `- [x] ${item}`).join('\n'))

Reviewed-by: adversarial-review (PASS)"
```

**After commit:**
- Update BEADS task status: `bd close <wu-task-id> --reason "4-phase loop complete. PASS."`
- If this work unit has a **human checkpoint** flag, pause and report before continuing
- Update the **Project Context Document** with completed work unit details

**After commit, update SERVICE-INVENTORY.md:**
If this work unit created or modified services, factories, database tables, or shared modules, update `SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` with the new entries. This document is read by subsequent coder agents to avoid duplicating existing services.

## 4. Quality Gate Enforcement

Quality gates are BLOCKING STATE TRANSITIONS, not advisory recommendations. The orchestrator CANNOT advance to the next phase without gate passage.

### State Machine

```text
IMPLEMENT в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ VALIDATE в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ REVIEW в”Җв”ҖвҶ’ COMMIT
                 в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
                 вҶ“            вҶ“
              FAIL:         FAIL:
           fix + re-run   fix + re-validate
                          + FRESH re-review
                 в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
              (max 3)      (max 3)
                 в”Ӯ            в”Ӯ
                 вҶ“            вҶ“
              ESCALATE     ESCALATE
             (to human)   (to human)
```

### Transition Rules (MUST, not SHOULD)

1. **IMPLEMENT вҶ’ VALIDATE**: Always. No exceptions.
2. **VALIDATE вҶ’ REVIEW**: ONLY if ALL validation checks pass:
   - Tests pass (exit code 0)
   - Coverage meets `.coverage-thresholds.json` thresholds
   - Type checking passes
   - Lint passes
   - File scope respected
3. **REVIEW вҶ’ COMMIT**: ONLY if adversarial review returns PASS
4. **FAIL вҶ’ retry**: Fix the issue, then re-run the FAILED gate (not skip it)
5. **Re-review after fix**: MUST spawn a FRESH reviewer (new instance, no memory)
6. **Max retries**: 3 attempts per gate, then ESCALATE to human with full failure history

### On FAIL: Mandatory Re-Review Protocol

1. Fix the issue identified by the reviewer
2. Re-run Phase 2 (VALIDATE) вҖ” ALL quality gates, not just the one that failed
3. **MANDATORY**: Spawn a NEW adversarial reviewer (fresh instance, no memory of previous review)
4. Only COMMIT after the fresh reviewer returns PASS
5. Max 3 retry cycles before ESCALATE to human

Track each attempt visibly: "Re-review attempt 1/3", "Re-review attempt 2/3", etc.

### What the Orchestrator MUST NOT Do

- "Coverage is close enough at 92%, proceeding to commit"
- "Adversarial review found issues but they're minor, committing anyway"
- "Fix applied, skipping re-review since the fix is straightforward"
- "5 FAILs encountered, moving to next work unit without resolution"
- "Tests pass but coverage command failed вҖ” proceeding anyway"

### What the Orchestrator MUST Do

- "VALIDATION FAIL: coverage at 87%, threshold is 100%. Returning to IMPLEMENT."
- "ADVERSARIAL REVIEW FAIL. Spawning fresh reviewer for re-review. Attempt 2 of 3."
- "Max retries (3) exceeded for WU-004. Escalating to human with failure history."
- "Fix applied. Re-running validation. Re-running adversarial review with FRESH reviewer."

## 5. Parallel Work Unit Execution

When multiple work units have no dependencies on each other, execute them in parallel вҖ” but with structured convergence points.

```text
              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-001: IMPLEMENT в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
Fan-out в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-002: IMPLEMENT в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Converge for VALIDATE
              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-003: IMPLEMENT в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                           в”Ӯ
              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-001: REVIEW в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
Fan-out в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-002: REVIEW в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Sequential COMMIT
              в”Ӯ                            в”Ӯ
              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ WU-003: REVIEW в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

**Rules for parallel execution:**

1. **Fan-out implementations**: Spawn coding subagents for independent work units simultaneously
2. **Converge for validation**: Wait for ALL parallel implementations to complete, then validate each
3. **Fan-out reviews**: Spawn review subagents for each work unit simultaneously
4. **Sequential commits**: Commit work units one at a time to maintain clean git history
5. **If any FAIL**: Only re-run the failed work unit's loop вҖ” don't re-run passed units

**Stale notification handling:** When parallel subagent results arrive after the orchestrator has moved past their work unit (e.g., at a checkpoint), acknowledge them briefly in one line. Do NOT print a full "still waiting at checkpoint" block for each stale notification вҖ” this clutters the conversation and wastes context window.

### Persisting to Disk (MANDATORY)

# Create directory if needed

# Write/update after each Phase 4 (COMMIT) and at orchestration start

```

**When to write:**
- At orchestration start (initial creation)
- After each Phase 4 (COMMIT) вҖ” add the completed work unit
- After each Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT) вҖ” update patterns if new ones emerge

**The file is NOT committed to git** during execution вҖ” it's a working document. It gets cleaned up after the PR is created (or left for the next session if interrupted).
